APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper fits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it tackles a well-motivated NLP problem (document clustering) using contemporary ML tools (word embeddings + optimal transport) and claims concrete algorithmic and systems contributions (modified B-ADMM, parallelization, Elkan-style pruning) that address prior scalability barriers. The experimental protocol meets community standards: multiple public datasets (Reuters, 20News, Ohsumed, BBC variants), a broad set of baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, LDA, NMF, PV, spectral methods), standard clustering metrics (V-measure, AMI, ARI), hyperparameter reporting, and provided code/datasets for reproducibility. From an experimental-methods perspective the work is aimed at the right audience and is positioned properly with respect to related work on EMD-based comparisons (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015), so its empirical and engineering claims are appropriate for a top NLP/ML venue. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top NLP/ML venues: it tackles document representation and clustering using Wasserstein distances over word-embedding supports, a topic of clear interest to both communities. The formulation (D2-clustering with Wasserstein barycenters and a scalable B-ADMM/parallel implementation) and the evaluation (multiple standard corpora, strong baselines, AMI/ARI/V-measure, and released code) match the methodological and reproducibility expectations of the venue. The audience (researchers working on embedding-based text comparison, optimal transport, and scalable algorithms) is appropriate, and the paper is positioned well relative to prior EMD-based work (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015) while addressing practical scalability—making it a good fit for a top conference.

The experimental protocol meets community standards: multiple public datasets (Reuters, 20News, Ohsumed, BBC variants), a broad set of baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, LDA, NMF, PV, spectral methods), standard clustering metrics (V-measure, AMI, ARI), hyperparameter reporting, and provided code/datasets for reproducibility. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies the now-established idea of representing documents as distributions over word embeddings and using optimal-transport (Wasserstein) distances, but combines this with a K-means–style D2-clustering framework and engineering-level innovations (a modified B-ADMM for barycenters, data-parallel implementation, and Elkan-style pruning). These algorithmic extensions appear non-trivial and the authors clearly differentiate their work from Kusner et al. (2015) and prior EMD-based comparisons, but the core problem formulation and use of Wasserstein distances are well-studied. From my experimental-methods perspective the contribution reads as an incremental yet useful advance in scalability and practical clustering, rather than a fundamentally new idea that would surprise experts. | B(3): The paper brings a moderate but meaningful novelty by adapting D2‑clustering (Wasserstein‑barycenter K‑means) to large document collections through a modified B‑ADMM solver, data‑parallel implementation, and pruning heuristics — an engineering and algorithmic extension beyond prior EMD/Word Mover’s Distance work (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015). The problem formulation (documents as discrete distributions over embeddings) is well‑studied, but the paper clearly differentiates its contributions around scalable barycenter computation and clustering efficiency. The techniques are non‑trivial algorithmic/implementation extensions rather than fundamentally new theory, so the contribution is an incremental-to-moderate advance that would be of interest to practitioners and researchers focused on scalable OT methods for NLP.

These algorithmic extensions appear non-trivial and the authors clearly differentiate their work from Kusner et al. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides a broad empirical evaluation across multiple standard datasets, many baselines, and several clustering metrics, with code and hyperparameters released — supporting the main claims about effectiveness and practical scalability. However, from the summary I see only limited ablations isolating which components drive gains (e.g., the modified B‑ADMM barycenter solver vs representation effects, or comparisons of parallel scaling and wall‑clock/memory costs), and experiments on domain shifts (Ohsumed) appear underpowered. Related work and limitations are discussed, but deeper sensitivity analyses and more detailed computational profiling would strengthen reproducibility and claims about scalability. | B(4): The paper evaluates the proposed D2-clustering across a broad set of datasets (BBCNews, Wiki events, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, BBCSports, Ohsumed) and against many competitive baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, PV, LDA, NMF, spectral methods), with multiple clustering metrics reported and code plus preprocessed data and hyperparameters released, supporting reproducibility. The authors report robustness to hyperparameter choices, and address scalability via a modified B-ADMM, parallelization, and pruning, but the summary gives limited detail on systematic ablation studies (e.g., isolating effects of barycenter solver choices or embedding variants) and on comprehensive failure-mode analysis beyond Ohsumed/domain-embedding caveats. Related work is described as adequate and known limitations (historical LP cost, KNN-graph issues) are acknowledged, so while the empirical evaluation is strong, a few targeted sensitivity/ablation analyses would make the evidence fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a broad empirical evaluation across multiple standard datasets, many baselines, and several clustering metrics, with code and hyperparameters released — supporting the main claims about effectiveness and practical scalability. However, from the summary I see only limited ablations isolating which components drive gains (e.g., the modified B‑ADMM barycenter solver vs representation effects, or comparisons of parallel scaling and wall‑clock/memory costs), and experiments on domain shifts (Ohsumed) appear underpowered. Related work and limitations are discussed, but deeper sensitivity analyses and more detailed computational profiling would strengthen reproducibility and claims about scalability.

The authors report robustness to hyperparameter choices, and address scalability via a modified B-ADMM, parallelization, and pruning, but the summary gives limited detail on systematic ablation studies (e.g., isolating effects of barycenter solver choices or embedding variants) and on comprehensive failure-mode analysis beyond Ohsumed/domain-embedding caveats. Related work is described as adequate and known limitations (historical LP cost, KNN-graph issues) are acknowledged, so while the empirical evaluation is strong, a few targeted sensitivity/ablation analyses would make the evidence fully comprehensive.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against a broad and appropriate set of baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, PV, LDA, NMF, spectral methods, etc.) and compares using standard clustering metrics (V-measure, AMI, ARI) on multiple well-known datasets with hyperparameters reported and code released, which supports reasonably fair comparisons. The authors explicitly discuss prior EMD-based work (Kusner et al. 2015) and clearly describe algorithmic differences (B-ADMM, data-parallel implementation, Elkan pruning) and computational trade-offs, strengthening the empirical claims. However, the summary does not indicate any explicit acknowledgment where a prior method outperforms theirs or detailed ablations isolating representation vs. algorithmic contributions, and some domain-specific baselines (e.g., specialized embeddings for Ohsumed) appear not fully explored. Overall the comparisons are strong and reproducible but miss a few targeted baselines/ablation analyses that would make the evaluation fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of standard and embedding-based baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, PV, LDA, NMF, spectral methods, etc.) across multiple public datasets using standard clustering metrics, which supports fair head-to-head evaluation. It explicitly contrasts its method with prior EMD-based work (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015) and discusses scalability limitations, and provides code and reproducible setups. However, while related work coverage is described as adequate and limitations are acknowledged, the summary suggests only minor omissions in benchmarking breadth (e.g., no mention of very recent learned-transport or contextual-embedding baselines), so comparisons are strong but not fully exhaustive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against a broad and appropriate set of baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, PV, LDA, NMF, spectral methods, etc.) and compares using standard clustering metrics (V-measure, AMI, ARI) on multiple well-known datasets with hyperparameters reported and code released, which supports reasonably fair comparisons. The authors explicitly discuss prior EMD-based work (Kusner et al. 2015) and clearly describe algorithmic differences (B-ADMM, data-parallel implementation, Elkan pruning) and computational trade-offs, strengthening the empirical claims. However, the summary does not indicate any explicit acknowledgment where a prior method outperforms theirs or detailed ablations isolating representation vs. algorithmic contributions, and some domain-specific baselines (e.g., specialized embeddings for Ohsumed) appear not fully explored. Overall the comparisons are strong and reproducible but miss a few targeted baselines/ablation analyses that would make the evaluation fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of standard and embedding-based baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, PV, LDA, NMF, spectral methods, etc.) across multiple public datasets using standard clustering metrics, which supports fair head-to-head evaluation. It explicitly contrasts its method with prior EMD-based work (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015) and discusses scalability limitations, and provides code and reproducible setups. However, while related work coverage is described as adequate and limitations are acknowledged, the summary suggests only minor omissions in benchmarking breadth (e.g., no mention of very recent learned-transport or contextual-embedding baselines), so comparisons are strong but not fully exhaustive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper presents a coherent framework and provides code and datasets, with extensive empirical comparisons against reasonable baselines and multiple clustering metrics, supporting the main claims. Mathematical components (Wasserstein barycenters and modified B-ADMM) appear plausible in the summary, but the paper would benefit from clearer theoretical guarantees and a more detailed complexity/wall-clock analysis to substantiate scalability claims. Experimental methodology is strong in breadth, but I see missing ablations to isolate gains from embedding choices versus the D2-clustering algorithm itself, and no mention of statistical significance tests. These are minor issues that do not overturn the main conclusions but should be addressed for full technical confidence. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments across multiple datasets showing consistent gains over reasonable baselines and by providing code for reproducibility. The methodological description (Wasserstein barycenters via a modified B-ADMM, data-parallel implementation, and Elkan-style pruning) is plausible and addresses the known computational bottleneck of LP-based EMD; no obvious mathematical errors are indicated in the summary. Some concerns remain about the strength of the scalability claim relative to Kusner et al. (2015) — the summary's critique of KNN-graph approaches and the curse-of-dimensionality argument may be somewhat overstated without more detailed comparative complexity analysis — and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing. Overall the technical contribution appears sound with only minor gaps in comparative analysis and reported statistical rigor.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper presents a coherent framework and provides code and datasets, with extensive empirical comparisons against reasonable baselines and multiple clustering metrics, supporting the main claims. Mathematical components (Wasserstein barycenters and modified B-ADMM) appear plausible in the summary, but the paper would benefit from clearer theoretical guarantees and a more detailed complexity/wall-clock analysis to substantiate scalability claims. Experimental methodology is strong in breadth, but I see missing ablations to isolate gains from embedding choices versus the D2-clustering algorithm itself, and no mention of statistical significance tests. These are minor issues that do not overturn the main conclusions but should be addressed for full technical confidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments across multiple datasets showing consistent gains over reasonable baselines and by providing code for reproducibility. The methodological description (Wasserstein barycenters via a modified B-ADMM, data-parallel implementation, and Elkan-style pruning) is plausible and addresses the known computational bottleneck of LP-based EMD; no obvious mathematical errors are indicated in the summary. Some concerns remain about the strength of the scalability claim relative to Kusner et al. (2015) — the summary's critique of KNN-graph approaches and the curse-of-dimensionality argument may be somewhat overstated without more detailed comparative complexity analysis — and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing. Overall the technical contribution appears sound with only minor gaps in comparative analysis and reported statistical rigor.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles an important practical bottleneck—making Wasserstein/EMD-based document clustering computationally practical at scale—by proposing a modified B-ADMM barycenter solver, data-parallel implementation and pruning, and demonstrating consistent improvements over a broad set of baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, PV, LDA, NMF, etc.) on multiple datasets (BBCNews, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, Ohsumed, etc.). From an experimental-methods perspective the contribution is impactful because it provides reproducible code and processed data, shows robustness to hyperparameters, and directly addresses prior scalability limitations (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015), so researchers and practitioners can realistically apply OT-based document comparison. While it is unlikely to be transformative to the entire field (OT in NLP was already established), it is a strong, broadly useful advance that will enable follow-on work and adoption in applied settings. | B(4): The paper addresses an important bottleneck—making Wasserstein/EMD-based document comparison and clustering computationally practical—by combining a modified B-ADMM barycenter solver, parallelization, and assignment pruning, and provides code and reproducible experiments showing consistent gains over standard baselines. By demonstrating scalability and robustness across multiple datasets (BBCNews, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, Ohsumed, etc.), it is likely to change how researchers evaluate embedding-aware clustering and encourage broader adoption of OT-based document representations. The work opens clear follow-ons (better domain embeddings, learned transport, end-to-end models) and thus has the potential for strong influence beyond this specific algorithmic contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important practical bottleneck—making Wasserstein/EMD-based document clustering computationally practical at scale—by proposing a modified B-ADMM barycenter solver, data-parallel implementation and pruning, and demonstrating consistent improvements over a broad set of baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, PV, LDA, NMF, etc.) on multiple datasets (BBCNews, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, Ohsumed, etc.). From an experimental-methods perspective the contribution is impactful because it provides reproducible code and processed data, shows robustness to hyperparameters, and directly addresses prior scalability limitations (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015), so researchers and practitioners can realistically apply OT-based document comparison. While it is unlikely to be transformative to the entire field (OT in NLP was already established), it is a strong, broadly useful advance that will enable follow-on work and adoption in applied settings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important bottleneck—making Wasserstein/EMD-based document comparison and clustering computationally practical—by combining a modified B-ADMM barycenter solver, parallelization, and assignment pruning, and provides code and reproducible experiments showing consistent gains over standard baselines. By demonstrating scalability and robustness across multiple datasets (BBCNews, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, Ohsumed, etc.), it is likely to change how researchers evaluate embedding-aware clustering and encourage broader adoption of OT-based document representations. The work opens clear follow-ons (better domain embeddings, learned transport, end-to-end models) and thus has the potential for strong influence beyond this specific algorithmic contribution.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a logical progression from problem motivation through method (D2-clustering and modified B-ADMM) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early and repeatedly. Notation appears consistent and definitions are provided for Wasserstein barycenters and the clustering framework; figures/tables and dataset/baseline descriptions effectively support the narrative. Writing is generally clear and grammatically sound, with hyperparameters and code release improving reproducibility. A few sections (e.g., algorithmic/complexity discussion and some experimental details) could be tightened or summarized more concisely to reach exceptional clarity. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and follows a clear flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion; main contributions are stated early and the code and datasets are provided which aids reproducibility. Notation appears consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables and examples meaningfully support the empirical claims across multiple datasets and baselines. Writing quality is good with limited ambiguity and adequate related-work coverage, though a few methodological sections (e.g., detailed B-ADMM derivations and some experimental settings) could be tightened for even clearer exposition. Overall the presentation is strong with only minor clarity refinements needed.

main_claims: Proposes a scalable D2-clustering framework that treats documents as discrete distributions over word embeddings and uses Wasserstein (EMD) distance for clustering.

Demonstrates that the proposed approach (using modified B-ADMM and parallelization) is computationally practical and more scalable than prior EMD-based document comparison methods (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015).
Shows empirically that D2-clustering consistently outperforms several baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, LDA, NMF, PV, etc.) across multiple datasets and is robust to hyperparameter choices.
Provides code and pre-processed datasets to reproduce experiments.
_summary.methodology: Represent each document as a discrete distribution over word embedding support points and perform D2-clustering (a K-means–like algorithm) where cluster centroids are Wasserstein barycenters computed via a modified Bregman ADMM algorithm with data-parallel implementations and Elkan-style pruning in assignment steps.
limitations: Original D2-clustering involved very high computational cost (large-scale LP) limiting scalability (stated as a historical limitation).
The KNN-graph prefetch-and-prune approach of Kusner et al. (2015) is stated to have limited scalability and to suffer from the curse of dimensionality.
Experiments on the Ohsumed dataset were limited, and better domain-specific embeddings may be needed for domain-specific corpora.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - document clustering using word embeddings and optimal transport (Wasserstein distance)

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methods Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating large-scale NLP systems, specializing in empirical methodology, benchmarking, and reproducible research tooling.
traits: Carefully checks experimental design: datasets, splits, hyperparameter tuning procedures, and significance of improvements
Values thorough ablations that isolate contributions (e.g., algorithmic changes vs. representation effects)
Insists on clear computational/complexity analysis and realistic runtimes (wall-clock, memory) for claimed scalability
Prefers reproducibility: expects code, dataset preprocessing, and exact training/evaluation recipes to be available and documented
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments and implementation justify the claims, recommending specific additional experiments or clarifications to make results trustworthy and reproducible.

reviewer_b.name: NLP Researcher — Embeddings & Optimal Transport
reviewer_b.background: Academic researcher with a track record in NLP and optimal transport methods, familiar with embedding-based document comparison, barycenters, and related literature on Earth Mover's Distance in text.
traits: Assesses novelty relative to prior work and whether methodological changes are conceptually incremental or substantial
Evaluates the broader impact and applicability: when and why the proposed approach should be preferred over simpler alternatives
Notices connections to related directions (e.g., learned transport, contextual embeddings, end-to-end models) and opportunities for future work
Values clarity on theoretical assumptions and limitations (e.g., sensitivity to embedding quality, document length, vocabulary overlap)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on the paper's position in the research landscape and its potential scientific and practical impact, recommending refinements to claims and pointers to related work or extensions.
